title: Enlargement and similar figures
source: Essential Mathematics for the Australian Curriculum, Year 9 (Cambridge, Greenwood et al. 2024) — Chapter 7 Geometry, Section 7F. Images are direct crops of the original textbook pages.

walkthrough.prompt: Part a — Find the scale factor from Original to Image.
walkthrough.reasoning: To find a scale factor, choose a pair of corresponding sides (one on each figure) and divide the image length by the original length.
The 4 cm side on the Original corresponds to the 6 cm side on the Image: 6 ÷ 4 = 1.5.

walkthrough.prompt: Part b — Find the value of $x$.
walkthrough.reasoning: x is on the Image, corresponding to the 3 cm side on the Original. Multiply the original length by the scale factor to get the image length.

walkthrough.prompt: Part c — Find the value of $y$.
walkthrough.reasoning: y is on the Original, corresponding to the 7.5 cm side on the Image. Multiplying y by the scale factor should give the image length.
Divide both sides by 1.5 to make y the subject.

questions.topic: identifying corresponding angles, sides and scale factor in similar figures
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): ∠F

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: matching up vertices/sides in the wrong order (e.g. matching AB with GH instead of AB with EF)
misconceptions.reason: corresponding parts must be matched in the same order the shapes are named/labelled - matching the wrong pairs gives a meaningless ratio

questions.topic: measuring an enlargement to find the scale factor
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): double (OA′ = 2 × OA)

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: measuring from the wrong point, or assuming the centre of enlargement is always inside the shape
misconceptions.reason: the centre of enlargement (O) can be inside, outside, or on the figure - distances must be measured from O to each vertex, not between vertices

questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): yes

misconceptions.reason: in similar figures, corresponding ANGLES are equal but corresponding SIDES are only in the same ratio (proportional), not equal, unless the scale factor is 1

questions.topic: explaining why certain shapes are, or are not, always similar
questions.answer: all angles of any square equal 90°, with only 1 side length (so any two squares are automatically similar)
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): all angles of any square equal 90°, with only 1 side length (so any two squares are automatically similar)

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: assuming corresponding sides are equal instead of just corresponding angles
misconceptions.reason: in similar figures, corresponding ANGLES are equal but corresponding SIDES are only in the same ratio (proportional), not equal, unless the scale factor is 1

questions.topic: explaining why certain shapes are, or are not, always similar
questions.answer: all angles in any equilateral triangle equal 60°, with only 1 side length (so any two equilateral triangles are automatically similar)
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): all angles in any equilateral triangle equal 60°, with only 1 side length (so any two equilateral triangles are automatically similar)

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: assuming corresponding sides are equal instead of just corresponding angles
misconceptions.reason: in similar figures, corresponding ANGLES are equal but corresponding SIDES are only in the same ratio (proportional), not equal, unless the scale factor is 1

questions.topic: explaining why certain shapes are, or are not, always similar
questions.answer: length and width might be multiplied by different numbers (so the angles could stay 90° while the ratio of length to width differs between the two rectangles)

questions.topic: explaining why certain shapes are, or are not, always similar
questions.answer: two isosceles triangles do not have to have the same base angles (the equal-sides could be a different proportion of the base)

questions.topic: the Sierpinski triangle - repeated enlargement and self-similar patterns
questions.answer: N/A (drawing task - continue the pattern for two more figures, each made of 3 shrunk copies of the previous figure)

questions.topic: the Sierpinski triangle - repeated enlargement and self-similar patterns
questions.answer: i  l/2   ii  l/4   iii  l/128 (each figure the smallest triangle is half the side length of the previous figure's smallest triangle)
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): i  l/2   ii  l/4   iii  l/128 (each figure the smallest triangle is half the side length of the previous figure's smallest triangle)

questions.topic: the Sierpinski triangle - repeated enlargement and self-similar patterns
questions.answer: i  3/4   ii  9/16   iii  243/1024 (each figure shades 3/4 of the previously shaded fraction, since 1 of every 4 triangles becomes unshaded)
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): i  3/4   ii  9/16   iii  243/1024 (each figure shades 3/4 of the previously shaded fraction, since 1 of every 4 triangles becomes unshaded)

questions.topic: the Sierpinski triangle - repeated enlargement and self-similar patterns
questions.answer: zero (as the enlargement and copying continues forever, the shaded area shrinks towards zero even though the pattern never stops)
